phrases.text_local: Une part de socca, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: oon PAR deh so-KAH seel voo PLAY
phrases.cultural_note: Chez Thérésa at Cours Saleya is one of Nice's most famous socca vendors — crowds gather when the pan comes out.

phrases.intent_en: Ask if the socca is freshly made right now
phrases.text_local: La socca vient-elle juste d'être préparée ?
phrases.pronunciation: lah so-KAH vyan-TEL ZHOOST DET-reh pray-pah-RAY
phrases.cultural_note: Socca is best eaten the moment it comes out of the wood oven — crispy edges, soft centre, dusted with black pepper.

responses.english: Yes, it is fresh from the oven.

phrases.intent_en: Ask if they also serve pissaladière
phrases.cultural_note: Pissaladière is a Niçois onion tart topped with anchovies and black olives — the local equivalent of pizza.

phrases.intent_en: Ask what drink goes well with socca
phrases.text_local: Quelle boisson accompagne bien la socca, selon vous ?
phrases.pronunciation: KEL bwah-SON ah-kom-PAN-yeh BYAN lah so-KAH seh-LON voo
phrases.cultural_note: A glass of chilled Niçois Bellet rosé or a local beer pairs perfectly with hot socca on a sunny day.

phrases.intent_en: Ask where to eat while standing at the market
phrases.text_local: Où peut-on manger debout ici, sur le marché ?
phrases.pronunciation: oo puh-TON man-ZHAY deh-BOO ee-SEE soor leh mar-SHAY
phrases.cultural_note: Socca is traditionally eaten standing up at the market, wrapped in paper — part of the authentic Niçois experience.

responses.english: Over there, there are high tables.
responses.phonetic: lah-BAH eel ee ah day TAHBL OHT
responses.french: Là-bas, il y a des tables hautes.
